The Limits of the Filter

This essay extends the argument begun earlier. The filter model was built to
explain how irrelevant material is excluded. It was not built to explain how
relevant material is selected when relevance itself is uncertain. That second
problem is harder, and it has been neglected.

Consider a reader scanning a crowded page. The eyes move in short jumps,
fixating briefly, then leaping ahead. Between fixations, little is encoded. The
text that falls outside the narrow window of high acuity is blurred, and yet
the reader rarely notices the blur. This is because the visual system fills in
the gaps from expectation, and expectation is shaped by context.

In a series of careful studies, words were briefly flashed and then masked.
When the words were predictable from the surrounding sentence, they were
recognized faster. When they were anomalous, recognition was slowed and errors
were more frequent. These results were replicated across languages, and the
effect was robust. The conclusion was straightforward. Prediction is not a
luxury layered atop perception; prediction is perception, run forward in time.

This is the thesis. Attention is not a single gate but a continuous wager on
what comes next, and the wager is settled, again and again, against the
incoming evidence.
